channel.title: Follow Builders RSS
channel.description: 聚合 AI 领域建设者的 X、播客和博客内容。

item.title: Once you use HTML to make slides, you simply can't go back Made with the Frontend Slides feature in @AnyGenIO https://t.co/yXEqQW1R2K
item.description:
作者：Zara Zhang 账号：@zarazhangrui 简介：Builder. Dangerously skips permissions. Harvard’17. GitHub: https://t.co/KCuEajezlL YouTube: https://t.co/8xzbGWtf6w

Made with the Frontend Slides feature in @AnyGenIO https://t.co/yXEqQW1R2K

item.title: You can also set your autocompact threshold yourself and effectively lower your context window if you'd prefer. For example, 400k context is a good compromise: CLAUDE_CODE_AUTO_COMPACT_WINDOW=400000 claude see docs here: https://t.co/NuhBuTSSDt
item.description:
作者：Thariq 账号：@trq212 简介：Claude Code @anthropicai. prev YC W20, mit media lab.
towards machines of loving grace
You can also set your autocompact threshold yourself and effectively lower your context window if you'd prefer.
For example, 400k context is a good compromise:
CLAUDE_CODE_AUTO_COMPACT_WINDOW=400000 claude
see docs here: https://t.co/NuhBuTSSDt

item.title: I edited the intro because I realized I buried the lede originally- The 1M context window is a double-edged sword. It allows Claude to do more complex tasks but it can also leads to more context pollution if you don't manage your session well. This is how you do that: https://t.co/nVWS6cIwO2
item.description:
作者：Thariq 账号：@trq212 简介：Claude Code @anthropicai. prev YC W20, mit media lab.
towards machines of loving grace
I edited the intro because I realized I buried the lede originally-
The 1M context window is a double-edged sword. It allows Claude to do more complex tasks but it can also leads to more context pollution if you don't manage your session well.
This is how you do that: https://t.co/nVWS6cIwO2

item.title: Why will AI create more jobs in plenty of industries? It’s because we’re going to use AI to accelerate output in one area, and then eventually you run into a new bottleneck somewhere else in the process that still requires humans. This example from the FT is an obvious one. More people asking legal...
item.description:
作者：Aaron Levie 账号：@levie 简介：ceo @box - your business lives in content. unleash it with AI
Why will AI create more jobs in plenty of industries? It’s because we’re going to use AI to accelerate output in one area, and then eventually you run into a new bottleneck somewhere else in the process that still requires humans.
This example from the FT is an obvious one. More people asking legal questions from AI agents, which downstream eventually will mean there are more lawyers being pinged with questions. There are other drivers, too, like AI accelerating new business formation, more patent filings, new scientific research, and so on - all of which eventually land in the laps of lawyers and other regulatory functions.
But the analogy holds for plenty of other work. More code will mean more security risks, which means more security researchers. Automating patient referrals in healthcare just leads to a bottleneck of not having enough doctors. More customer outreach via AI leads to more sales conversations. You can list thousands of categories like this.
There’s a lot of areas where AI will lead to “efficiency” in the sense that we will automate something and then spend less in that area.
But the value proposition taps out at some point because the world isn’t static. Your competitor will use AI to build a better product, go out and meet with even more customers, deliver a better service, run better ad campaigns, and you eventually have to match them or die.

item.title: in the grand narrative of Meta x AI, we saw the flop (Llama 4 hurhurhur), and now we’re seeing the turn: - *more* hiring since the soup wars of 2025 - Zuck literally moved in with Alexandr and Nat and is koding again - finally GA’ed Opus-ish level model (no api, not open, but still) - bought @dps ...
item.description:
作者：Swyx 账号：@swyx 简介：achieve ambition with intentionality, intensity, integrity & insanity.
affiliations: - @dxtipshq - @cognition - @temporalio - @aidotengineer - @latentspacepod
in the grand narrative of Meta x AI, we saw the flop (Llama 4 hurhurhur), and now we’re seeing the turn:
- *more* hiring since the soup wars of 2025 - Zuck literally moved in with Alexandr and Nat and is koding again - finally GA’ed Opus-ish level model (no api, not open, but still) - bought @dps Dreamer and @peakji Manus to build the AI OS prosumer layer
the MSL “river” is gonna be pretty exciting.

item.title: Find someone who genuinely cares. A mentor. A founder. A VC. An advisor. A customer. Life’s too short to work with people who don’t give a sh!t. It might end up being the greatest moat of them all.
item.description:
作者：Nikunj Kothari 账号：@nikunj 简介：partner @fpvventures - investing in seed/A. previous: early hire @meter, @opendoor, @atlassian & others. love @shimoleejhaveri + 👦👧
Find someone who genuinely cares.
A mentor. A founder. A VC. An advisor. A customer.
Life’s too short to work with people who don’t give a sh!t.
It might end up being the greatest moat of them all.

item.title: I've commented that "this is the year of subagents", but that is largely an optimization problem. the inverse problem - having agents that compose and boss agents that manage/query them - is a capabilities one. as an advisor to cog, proud to have played a small part in designing the new Spaces con...
item.description:
作者：Swyx 账号：@swyx 简介：achieve ambition with intentionality, intensity, integrity & insanity.
affiliations: - @dxtipshq - @cognition - @temporalio - @aidotengineer - @latentspacepod
I've commented that "this is the year of subagents", but that is largely an optimization problem.
the inverse problem - having agents that compose and boss agents that manage/query them - is a capabilities one.
as an advisor to cog, proud to have played a small part in designing the new Spaces concept 3 months ago and today's launch is a start of even more to come. congrats to the team!

item.title: The roadmap for Claude Cowork is… one month long. Things are moving too fast in AI to plan any further. Ship, evaluate, iterate. @felixrieseberg, who heads up Cowork at @AnthropicAI https://t.co/8YYpbM2yXU https://t.co/ikBz3rW0sT
item.description:
作者：Matt Turck 账号：@mattturck 简介：VC at @FirstMarkCap. Host: MAD Podcast; Organizer: Data Driven NYC, Author: MAD Landscape.
The roadmap for Claude Cowork is… one month long.
Things are moving too fast in AI to plan any further. Ship, evaluate, iterate.
@felixrieseberg, who heads up Cowork at @AnthropicAI https://t.co/8YYpbM2yXU https://t.co/ikBz3rW0sT

item.title: I think “play” should be taken more seriously If you work in AI, playing with the models is literally part of your job By play I mean “testing the models’ limits in a non-utilitarian way, with a sense of playful exploration and experimentation” Not only is it NOT a waste of time, but I often find...
item.description:
作者：Zara Zhang 账号：@zarazhangrui 简介：Builder. Dangerously skips permissions. Harvard’17. GitHub: https://t.co/KCuEajezlL YouTube: https://t.co/8xzbGWtf6w
I think “play” should be taken more seriously
If you work in AI, playing with the models is literally part of your job
By play I mean “testing the models’ limits in a non-utilitarian way, with a sense of playful exploration and experimentation”
Not only is it NOT a waste of time, but I often find it to be the most productive part of my day when I look back

item.title: Wowza so many comments.. quick things: 1) You can give system diagrams to Claude code and definitely one shot a lot of this. The point is it takes a WHILE to get to this point of clarity that AI can’t do today 2)People quoting me the bitter lesson and long horizon tasks - yes, you could be right. ...
item.description:
作者：Nikunj Kothari 账号：@nikunj 简介：partner @fpvventures - investing in seed/A. previous: early hire @meter, @opendoor, @atlassian & others. love @shimoleejhaveri + 👦👧
Wowza so many comments.. quick things:
1) You can give system diagrams to Claude code and definitely one shot a lot of this. The point is it takes a WHILE to get to this point of clarity that AI can’t do today
2)People quoting me the bitter lesson and long horizon tasks - yes, you could be right. But AI hasn’t learned the lessons and clarity that you have. Surely, 5-10 years later with infinite compute and simulations, we could get there.
3) Ok fine the slack notification system was not the best example. But the ethos still stand. Clarity, depth of thinking and persistence still take time.

item.title: That was the case in December. 4 months and thousands of work hours later, we have a great security concept; you can go all yolo, use a sandbox (Docker or OpenShell), there are allow-lists and per-access exec allow/deny prompts. There’s hundreds of security researchers that pen-tested it.
item.description:
作者：Peter Steinberger 账号：@steipete 简介：Polyagentmorous ClawFather. Came back from retirement to mess with AI and help a lobster take over the world @openclaw🦞
That was the case in December. 4 months and thousands of work hours later, we have a great security concept; you can go all yolo, use a sandbox (Docker or OpenShell), there are allow-lists and per-access exec allow/deny prompts.
There’s hundreds of security researchers that pen-tested it.

everything is coding agent

item.title: This is what the future of design looks like. Not just this specific tool¹, but the fact that every team in the world is now is empowered to build their own 'design factory'. Shader Lab was built with Claude Code, @threejs, @nextjs, and @vercel. To the exact needs, vision, and specification of @bas...
item.description:
作者：Guillermo Rauch 账号：@rauchg 简介：@vercel CEO
This is what the future of design looks like. Not just this specific tool¹, but the fact that every team in the world is now is empowered to build their own 'design factory'.
Shader Lab was built with Claude Code, @threejs, @nextjs, and @vercel. To the exact needs, vision, and specification of @basementstudio.
Every time we work on a project with them, we get a glimpse of an arsenal of internal tools they've deployed. Some built specifically for a project, some more general purpose.
It's now easier to generate software re-assembling powerful building blocks, than searching and procuring the right SaaS for the job.
¹ though it's a banger

item.title: The arc of a LLM skeptic: 1. They are just auto-correct 2. They are just next-token predictors 3. They hallucinate 4. They can only be useful for the data they are trained on 5. They are only good for coding 6. They are only good for verifiable things Just admit it! LLMs are awesome.
item.description:
作者：Aditya Agarwal 账号：@adityaag 简介：General Partner @SouthPkCommons, Co-Founder @Bevel_Health | Ex: Early Eng @facebook, CTO @Dropbox, Board @Flipkart | Optimist, Builder, Dad
The arc of a LLM skeptic: 1. They are just auto-correct 2. They are just next-token predictors 3. They hallucinate 4. They can only be useful for the data they are trained on 5. They are only good for coding 6. They are only good for verifiable things
Just admit it! LLMs are awesome.

item.title: “Where do you run inference?” “allbirds” “The shoes?” “Yea” https://t.co/2DgPXr2rsw
item.description:
作者：Amjad Masad 账号：@amasad 简介：ceo @replit. civilizationist
“Where do you run inference?”
“allbirds”
“The shoes?”
“Yea” https://t.co/2DgPXr2rsw

item.title: If finding security flaws is fully automated with frontier models à la Mythos, then GitHub should have a metric, like stars, showing how much compute is spent securing/hardening an open-source package. Example: 📦 linus/linux ⭐️ 200k 🦾 $239M Only way OSS can be trusted.
item.description:
作者：Amjad Masad 账号：@amasad 简介：ceo @replit. civilizationist
If finding security flaws is fully automated with frontier models à la Mythos, then GitHub should have a metric, like stars, showing how much compute is spent securing/hardening an open-source package. Example:
📦 linus/linux ⭐️ 200k 🦾 $239M
Only way OSS can be trusted.

item.title: Feels like there's an outage for Claude every other day - I wonder if this is related to the pace of shipping, just scaling compute, or something else?
item.description:
作者：Peter Yang 账号：@petergyang 简介：I share extremely practical AI tutorials and interviews | Join 140K+ readers at https://t.co/XYKTmGVH14 | Product at Roblox
Feels like there's an outage for Claude every other day - I wonder if this is related to the pace of shipping, just scaling compute, or something else?

item.title: TASTE https://t.co/yqDynY1ji8
item.description:
作者：Nan Yu 账号：@thenanyu 简介：head of product @linear
TASTE https://t.co/yqDynY1ji8

item.title: The AI Model Built for What LLMs Can't Do
item.description:
播客：AI & I by Every
Speaker 1 | 00:00 - 00:01 Can you define EBM for us?
Speaker 2 | 00:01 - 00:20 EBMs are naturally non autoregressive. There are no sequences of tokens, and that's what makes it fundamentally different. Like, imagine you're trying to navigate the map, and you have a left brain. To navigate, you're sort of allowed to choose one direction of the time. And sometimes you take the wrong turns just because you hallucinate.
Speaker 2 | 00:20 - 00:34 Like, there might be a hole in the road and you're just gonna fall. And you might see this hole, but you cannot turn back because you're ultra regressive LLM. EBM gonna have the bird view all the time. So if you see there's a hole, you're gonna choose a different route.
Speaker 1 | 00:48 - 00:50 Eve, welcome to the show.
Speaker 2 | 00:50 - 00:52 Hi. Thanks for having me.
Speaker 1 | 00:52 - 01:00 Great to have you on. For people who don't know, you are the founder and CEO of Logical Intelligence. Tell us what Logical Intelligence does.
Speaker 2 | 01:01 - 01:32 So Logical Intelligence does few things. First of all, we see ourselves as a foundational AI company. So we work in both with EBMs and LLMs. So everything built in house, we prototyped on LLM initially and we're building EBM at the same time and that sort of gets plugged in in the long term. We focused on correctness of software and hardware as a product.
Speaker 2 | 01:33 - 01:48 Because I believe there is a lot of issues with AI being placed in mission critical systems today. Like, you know, can we do the code gen? Can we do the chip design? And the answer is yes. Yes, people use LLMs today.
Speaker 2 | 01:49 - 02:07 But very few are actually questioning of like how these results are actually, like, correct. Does it make sense what it produce? And it seems like there's a big gap on market today, having deterministic AI, verifiable AI. So we're trying to fill that gap.
Speaker 1 | 02:07 - 02:21 The place my brain goes first is why does correctness or whether something makes sense, why does that matter if it works?
Speaker 2 | 02:23 - 02:46 Actually, let me ask you a question back. So speaking of correctness, I don't know. Well, imagine there's AI driving a car and you are in that car and that car is an LLM. And someone tells you, like, you know, 20% of the time it's gonna hallucinate and you might end up, like, in in, like, wrong place, how would you feel about it?
Speaker 1 | 02:46 - 02:55 Well, I think in my in my case, I'd be like, wow. That's kinda interesting. I'm I'm curious where takes me. Oh, okay. Okay.
Speaker 1 | 02:55 - 02:55 Let me
Speaker 2 | 02:55 - 03:06 give you another example. Yeah. How about the plane? How about the plane? You take a plane from SF to New York and someone says, you know, like, 20% of the time it might just, like, the next word not gonna match and it's gonna go down.
Speaker 2 | 03:06 - 03:08 So how would you feel about it?
Speaker 1 | 03:08 - 03:17 Yeah. My my my feeling about that is planes are currently run very well by deterministic systems. So I don't know why I would need an AI for that.
Speaker 2 | 03:18 - 04:02 I feel like we just cannot avoid AI anywhere. Like next ten years, people are going to try to place AI everywhere, automate systems with AI and, you know, technically you might not need. We survived somewhat without AI up to this moment, but now it's just like a next step of evolution that people just want AI everywhere. Like for the banking, you don't need AI initially, but we learn it's really helpful to automate certain processes in decision making And it's gonna save us a lot of time and allow us space to be creative instead of, like, debugging and fixing things. So I just feel like it's an avoidable future.
Speaker 1 | 04:03 - 04:25 I think maybe what I'm getting at is what am I getting at? It seems like if you want a guarantee of certainty using the only way to sort of guarantee certainty is to use something that you can express in code or logic.
Speaker 2 | 04:26 - 04:50 Mhmm. That's a part of it. So the certainty comes from internal verifiers and external verifiers, at least for us. So, for example, if you take LLM, obviously, it's a language based model and architecture doesn't allow you to do internal verifiers. So you like it's like a black box for you.
Speaker 2 | 04:50 - 05:29 You don't have access to what's inside until it's all processed. But you have access to the output. And many people and companies sort of take LLM, train it for certain tasks. And if it requires logic, they attach texturing verifiers to it, such as languages like Lean4, which is a proof of, I mean, machine verifiable language, proof language, which allows you to check this output using mathematical frameworks. However, you know, it doesn't solve the problem of things being just so expensive.
Speaker 2 | 05:29 - 06:03 Because what's expensive is your architecture, which is still playing a guessing game out here. And even if you attach external verifier, even you fine tune this LLM specifically for the task you're trying to create, you're still not solving the problems of tokens being expensive. It takes compute for you to play a guessing game. So this problem is solved by the EBMs, but we're talking about LLMs for now. So here we have the situation when there is, right, internal absence of verifier, but there's external one.
Speaker 2 | 06:04 - 06:16 So now about the EBMs. EBMs don't have tokens. It's token free model. There's no guessing game of this kind. So essentially, you could oversee all the possible scenarios.
Speaker 1 | 06:16 - 06:18 Can you define EBM for us?
Speaker 2 | 06:18 - 06:44 Yeah, I'll define in a second. So for now, just think of it as something which doesn't play a guessing game and something which has architecture, which is essentially allow you to self align itself as it's processing the information. And it's no longer a black box for you. So as it's performing, you can open it any time during the training and you could see what's happening in there. So you cannot do this with LLMs.
Speaker 2 | 06:44 - 07:08 Just the nature of architecture is different. So you have for verification tasks, you have this notion of self alignment because of the EBM architecture and absence of token makes it cheap, but also you have external verifier on top of it. So you have verification sort of on both sides, inside and outside. Hopefully, makes sense.
Speaker 1 | 07:08 - 07:30 I think so. Let me let me play it back to you, and you tell me if if if I'm getting you. So, basically, I think what you're saying is we're living in this in this world, which is really cool with LLMs, which is we can generate lots of output with them. And the output is really useful for for a lot of different things. But in order to tell if the output is right, the the best we can do is sort of guess and check.
Speaker 1 | 07:30 - 07:54 We generate the output and then, for example, if it's code, then we go and check the check the code with integration tests or manual tests or whatever just to see if it works. And that totally works but it is expensive and time consuming. And one of the problems is it's very hard for us to know, okay, how did the LLM get to this answer? We can't like go look inside of it.
Speaker 2 | 07:54 - 07:54 Exactly.
Speaker 1 | 07:55 - 08:29 And I think what you're saying is there are other types of models that are a little bit more inspectable and that give us a sense before we even try the before we even try the output to understand does this work, does the output work, we can get a sense from the model by looking at its internals. So sort of like how good is this solution? How good does this model think this solution is? And it's sort of like being able to ask someone like, are you sure about this? Like, how how how good is this before you, like, go check their work?
Speaker 1 | 08:29 - 08:47 And a language model can answer that question, but a language model's answers are working at ...

item.title: If you look at GPT 5.4-Cyber and it's ability for closed source reverse engineering, I have bad news for you. I do very much feel the pain though, there's hundreds of teams that try to poke holes into @openclaw. Our response has been of rapid iteration and code hardening. Which did introduce occasi...
item.description:
作者：Peter Steinberger 账号：@steipete 简介：Polyagentmorous ClawFather. Came back from retirement to mess with AI and help a lobster take over the world @openclaw🦞
If you look at GPT 5.4-Cyber and it's ability for closed source reverse engineering, I have bad news for you.
I do very much feel the pain though, there's hundreds of teams that try to poke holes into @openclaw. Our response has been of rapid iteration and code hardening. Which did introduce occasiaonal regression (and yes you all been yelling at me), but I see as the only way forward.
I would be very careful of other open source projects/harnesses that ignore this work and do not publish their advisories. https://t.co/NBaCouMs3i

item.title: This radical pivot must have come after a lot of sole searching https://t.co/GH8juust1I
item.description:
作者：Matt Turck 账号：@mattturck 简介：VC at @FirstMarkCap. Host: MAD Podcast; Organizer: Data Driven NYC, Author: MAD Landscape.
This radical pivot must have come after a lot of sole searching https://t.co/GH8juust1I

item.title: The software factory is the product: https://t.co/OAbONaDZdy https://t.co/xtkwaIYanh
item.description:
作者：Guillermo Rauch 账号：@rauchg 简介：@vercel CEO
The software factory is the product: https://t.co/OAbONaDZdy https://t.co/xtkwaIYanh

item.title: I recently came back from a 2-week trip to China and it was eye-opening to see how the world's second largest economy operates. I think every product builder should visit at least once to understand: → Chinese AI work culture → Electric vehicles, $2 delivery, and more → How people in China live an...
item.description:
作者：Peter Yang 账号：@petergyang 简介：I share extremely practical AI tutorials and interviews | Join 140K+ readers at https://t.co/XYKTmGVH14 | Product at Roblox
I recently came back from a 2-week trip to China and it was eye-opening to see how the world's second largest economy operates.
I think every product builder should visit at least once to understand:
→ Chinese AI work culture → Electric vehicles, $2 delivery, and more → How people in China live and work
📌 Here are 15 observations from my visit: https://t.co/EzvVfnEz6K

item.title: once again, I’m amazed by scammers. https://t.co/IFJYN7EWMv
item.description:
作者：Peter Steinberger 账号：@steipete 简介：Polyagentmorous ClawFather. Came back from retirement to mess with AI and help a lobster take over the world @openclaw🦞
once again, I’m amazed by scammers. https://t.co/IFJYN7EWMv
